---
title: "Mosaic plots from scratch with ggplot2"
description: "Marimekko ab initio"
author:
  - name: Quang Nguyen
    url: https://github.com/qntkhvn
date: "May 17, 2026"
output:
  distill::distill_article:
    math_method: katex
    highlight: rstudio
    code_folding: true
    self_contained: false
categories:
  - data viz
  - ggplot2
  - mosaic plots
preview: mosaic.png
---

I'm currently teaching a [class](https://36-315-summer26.netlify.app/) on statistical graphics and visualization during the summer semester.
The class begins with common visualizations for categorical and quantitative data, before moving on to more complex data structures.

Over the last few days, I've been prepping materials for categorical data viz.
A common graphic for plotting two or more categorical variables is a [mosaic plot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mosaic_plot).
(I also just learned that mosaic plots are also called [Marimekko](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marimekko).)

Here is an example of a mosaic plot, taken from its [Wikipedia page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mosaic_plot). 
This is taken from [The Guardian's article](https://www.theguardian.com/news/datablog/2009/mar/20/1) on "1000 songs to hear before you die".

<center>
![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/84/Mosaic-big.png?20090726044211){height=500}
</center>

If you're new to mosaic plots:

* A mosaic plot is a spine plot of spine plots.

* Column widths represent the marginal distribution of a variable `x`.

* Within each column, rectangle heights represent a conditional distribution `y|x`.

* Rectangle area represents the joint distribution of the variables.

* Mosaic plots are useful for visualizing independence: if proportions are the same across groups, the boxes line up into a grid.

Anyway, let's try to recreate the mosaic plot above.

First, here are the data for plotting.

```{r}
library(tidyverse)

songs <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/qntkhvn/36-315-summer26/refs/heads/master/data/songs.csv") |> 
  mutate(decade = cut(YEAR, right = TRUE, breaks = c(-Inf, 1959, 1969, 1979, 1989, 1999, Inf),
                      labels = c("1910s-50s", "1960s", "1970s", "1980s", "1990s", "2000s")))
```

In base `R`, this is straightforward with `mosaicplot()`.
(In fact, I think they used this exact function to make the plot.)

```{r, fig.height=10, fig.width=12}
box_colors <- c("red", "gold1", "greenyellow", "lightgreen", "dodgerblue2", "blue3", "magenta2")
songs |> 
  select(decade, THEME) |> 
  table() |> 
  mosaicplot(cex = 1.2, las = 1, main = NULL, col = box_colors)
```

There are other packages for making mosaic plots in `R` such as [`vcd`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/vcd) and [`vcdExtra`](https://friendly.github.io/vcdExtra).

As a `ggplot2` guru, I of course prefer to make my data viz with `ggplot2` when possible.
For mosaic plots, the [`ggmosaic`](https://haleyjeppson.github.io/ggmosaic/) package has been around for awhile.
However, `ggmosaic` has had recurring issues and has periodically been archived from CRAN.

I also recently came across the [`marimekko`](https://gogonzo.github.io/marimekko/) package.
Still, I find the syntax (of both `ggmosaic` and `marimekko`) somewhat unintuitive.
For new users in particular, the highly specialized variable-mapping syntax can create a fairly steep learning curve.

So, why not just create a mosaic plot from scratch with `ggplot2`?
As it turns out, this is not that difficult.
(I'm sure I'm not the first person to do this.)

```{r, include=FALSE}
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))
```

```{r, fig.height=5.5, fig.width=7}
songs |> 
  count(decade, THEME) |>
  group_by(decade = str_c(decade, "\n")) |>
  mutate(decade_total = sum(n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(decade_total / 2, n, fill = THEME, width = decade_total)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill", color = "white", linewidth = 2, show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ decade, scales = "free_x", space = "free_x") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), breaks = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), 
                     breaks = c(0.03, 0.12, 0.35, 0.56, 0.67, 0.85, 0.96),
                     labels = rev(sort(unique(songs$THEME)))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("red", "gold1", "greenyellow", "lightgreen", "dodgerblue2", "blue3", "magenta2")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme(strip.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = NA),
        strip.text.x = element_text(color = "black"),
        strip.clip = "off",
        panel.grid = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.y = element_line(color = NA),
        axis.text.y = element_text(hjust = 0))
```

Let's break it down:

* I first calculated the total for each decade (`decade_total`) in order to size the x-axis.

* I created a stacked bar chart with `geom_col()`, where `x` is mapped to the center of the total (`decade_total / 2`), and `width` is mapped to `decade_total`.

* **The trick is here faceting. I faceted by `decade` and most importantly, specified `scales = "free_x"` and `space = "free_x"` to allow the panels to have different widths.**

* I then enhanced the plot by trimming off different axis elements and adding labels and colors.

* I also found out that specifying `strip.clip = "off"` within `theme()` enables strip text to run off the panel (see "1910s-50s" in the plot).

That's it!
